caught at end of turn — Claude doesn't see them until next turn. For long turns that introduce errors early and don't notice, that's a delay in catching them. I AM GUESSING the latency win outweighs this since Claude tends to run svelte-check anyway before claiming work done.

Order I'd apply: (a) first as a safety net, then (b) for the cascade fix, then (c) if you want maximum responsiveness.

settings.local.json: replace check-ts.sh in PostToolUse with mark-ts-check-pending.sh; add check-ts.sh to Stop alongside banned-words-check and phrase-check.

Net effect: per-edit latency drops to a single `touch` call (~10ms). svelte-check runs at most once per turn, only when .ts/.svelte was edited, and never blocks even on errors. Errors surface as context on the next turn.

Say "go" to apply.

custom force for the parts the library doesn't cover. The amount of project code that would actually be saved is small once the adaptation layer is written.
- d3-force expects to own the simulation loop. It has an asynchronous "tick" scheduler that fires callbacks. Running it synchronously inside a render pass works (call `simulation.tick()` N times) but goes against the library's intended usage.

custom force for the parts the library doesn't cover. The amount of project code that would actually be saved is small once the adaptation layer is written.
- d3-force expects to own the simulation loop. It has an asynchronous "tick" scheduler that fires callbacks. Running it synchronously inside a render pass works (call `simulation.tick()` N times) but goes against the library's intended usage.

the value of d3-force here is mostly "someone else wrote and tested the same dozen lines of math" — not access to algorithms you couldn't reproduce. For a project that has been keeping its dependency list minimal, the hand-rolled version is the more consistent choice; for a project that already uses d3-force or wants to standardize on it for other layouts down the line, the library version is.

Close. A ray has a starting point and a direction (the arrowhead shows the direction), but it does NOT stop where the arrowhead is — it keeps going forever in that direction. An arrow drawn on paper has a finite length; a ray is the line you'd get if you stretched that arrow infinitely past its head.

Updated. Three matching rules now:

1. Line is exactly `done` or `done.`
2. Line starts with `v:` and ends with `done`/`done.`
3. Line ends with `done.` (period required — keeps casual usage without period from firing)

Examples cover the three patterns plus the false-positive cases that still don't fire (`done deal`, `i am done` without period, `are you done?` with `?`).

Updated. Three matching rules now:

1. Line is exactly `done` or `done.`
2. Line starts with `v:` and ends with `done`/`done.`
3. Line ends with `done.` (period required — keeps casual usage without period from firing)

Examples cover the three patterns plus the false-positive cases that still don't fire (`done deal`, `i am done` without period, `are you done?` with `?`).

Updated. Rule 3 is now broader — `done.` (word followed by period) appearing anywhere in a line triggers the hook. Rules 1 (bare `done`) and 2 (`v: ... done`) still cover the no-period cases.

Examples now include `done. ready for the next item` and `we ran the test and we are done. and passing` to show the rule 3 mid-line behavior.

Plain English: hook is wired into the user-prompt-submit chain in the workspace settings. It reads the user's message, scans each line against the three rules, and outputs the checklist as JSON-wrapped additional context only when one of the rules matches. The next time you say "done" (or any of the matching variants), the checklist will appear in front of me automatically.

Locked in:

- Buffer from the combined drawing outline: 15 pixels.
- Buffer between any two labels: 33 pixels.
- Fireblock labels stay out of the search entirely — the existing first/last-only rule keeps deciding which ones draw, and they're treated as fixed obstacles by the search (every other label has to keep its 33-pixel distance from them).

ghest conflict count first, then (for the remaining ties) alphabetical by part ancestry path. The slot-picking step within each label picks the slot that conflicts with the fewest still-unplaced labels' remaining slots; if multiple slots tie, the one whose persisted choice from last paint matches wins, otherwise the one with the smallest silhouette clearance.

Patch both, change either, or leave?

ignment. Every tie-break — whether ordering labels, ordering slots, or seeding the stochastic step — uses a stated rule, with "alphabetical by part ancestry path" as the catch-all when no other rule applies. No randomness from a non-deterministic source.

That single rule pins determinism, gives a default tie-break that any sort step can fall back on, and rules out drift between runs.

ptimization. For 100 labels that's ~5000 pair-conflict checks per cold run. If each check costs even 50 microseconds, that alone is 250 milliseconds — ten times over budget. The pair check has to be cheap (closed form rather than iterative) or it has to be lazy (only check pairs the greedy seed actually puts close together). Probably worth pinning the per-pair check approach as part of rule 23.

r 2: ~750 pair survivors × 64 combinations × 1 closed-form rectangle test ≈ 48,000 operations. At 100 nanoseconds per test, about 5 milliseconds.
- Tier 3: only the truly-stubborn pairs (probably under 50), each gets a real search step.

Total well inside the 25-millisecond cold-run budget. I AM GUESSING on the survivor counts and the per-test cost — both want measurement before committing.

; if no single-label switch fixes the conflict, look for a two-label swap; cap at two labels moving at once.

- A child hidden by its parent's hide-children setting DOES block (its geometry is physically there).

ule:
1. Skip the occlusion check entirely when the render mode is line-drawing.
2. Always run it, but only count an occluder face when the renderer would actually draw that face as solid.

Both end up at the same answer for the simple case (whole scene wireframe vs whole scene solid). They differ if individual parts can opt into solid-or-wireframe independently.

Which way do you want it written?

- At the dim line end (the far end). Catches the image's case directly. Contradicts your "NOT further out".
- At BOTH ends — start near the anchor AND at the dim line end. Two measurements per pair.
- At the worst-case point along the length (solve for minimum). One measurement, mathematically guaranteed to catch all convergence cases.

Two ways to wire it:
- Reject the pair if the dim-line distance at witness-length-max is below the clearance. Simpler; rejects pairs that could have worked at shorter witness lengths.
- Cap witness-length-max at the value where the dim-line distance equals the clearance. More permissive; keeps the pair when a shorter witness works. Needs one quadratic solve.

** these symmetric cases are rare for world-parallel rays under normal camera angles, but they can come up.

Options when neither corner is obtuse (or both are):
- Pick the larger of the two angles regardless of acute/obtuse, and apply the same perpendicular construction.
- Compute the perpendicular from BOTH corners and take the smaller of the two distances.

   - Compute the 3D bounding box of the scene, then expand each face by a 3D distance chosen so the face projects exactly 15 px outside the projected silhouette. Recomputed each paint as the camera moves.
   - Pick a fixed 3D offset once (a constant in world units) and live with whatever pixel margin that gives. Stable across tumbles, less precise visually.

A fireblock is a generic gap-filler in the lexicon, but specifically a clone whose length differs from the template's in the dimensions glossary. Possibly the same thing, possibly different.

Want me to propose adding any of these to the lexicon, or to surface the mismatches for you to settle separately?

ee dashes on a line below. Inside live key-value facts about the file — its real title, tags, date, the name it should show in lists, whether it should be hidden, anything the writer wants the system to know. The actual body of the file starts after the closing three dashes.

Example:
```
---
title: Little Cloud Vineyard
tags: [home, intro]
hidden: false
---

The actual page body goes here.
```

Option A. The reader features the proposal cares about — collapsed sections, sidebar toggle, scroll position in the sidebar — all want state to survive a click. Full reload throws that state away on every navigation, then we either accept the loss or add local-storage persistence. The interceptor cost in Option A is small once the router piece exists, which it does.

Recommendation: Option A.

line that happens to be centered. Calling it a callout muddies what the word means. A future writer skimming the system would see "callouts include centered text" and wonder how those two things fit together. They do not, really.

So the syntax says "this is a callout" and the design says "no it is not." The word doing the work of carrying the meaning is the wrong word. That is the second fight.

The quote mark is what makes it a real callout, which is the form the centering was built to catch (the spec calls it "the `[!center]` callout type").

Corrected lines would be:

```
> [!center] # Little Cloud Vineyard

> [!center] ![[lcv.label.png]]
```

Want me to fix the home file, or would you rather I make the centering also accept the bare form with no quote mark in front?

In Obsidian, that same spot after the bar is a size: `![[photo.png|200]]` means 200 wide, and `![[photo.png|200x120]]` means 200 wide by 120 tall.

Proposal: when the part after the bar is a number (or number-by-number), treat it as the size and draw the image at that size; otherwise keep treating it as caption text as today. Plus a test for the new sizing.

nteraction. Labels do not jump from one face of the box to another as the camera turns a few degrees.
- Con: needs state across paints — remember the previous frame's pick so the next pick can prefer it. Today's picker is pure.
- Con: needs a definition of "small enough change" so the picker knows when to stick versus when to move. That cutoff is itself a tunable.
- Con: most code of the three.

tified.

If labels do jump too much once the new path is on by default, add stability as a tie-breaker on top of closest, so closest stays the primary rule.

Reason for not picking least-crowded first: its main win is reducing the packing job in step 3b, not the visual feel. And it is order-dependent, which is a real correctness wart — the same scene can look different depending on label order.

ext to name the current mode.

Three options for the label:

a. "uniface on ⟳" / "uniface off ⟳" — most literal, no ambiguity.
b. "uniface ⟳" / "old dim ⟳" — names the mode that is currently active. Matches the pattern of "2D"/"3D" and "solid"/"x-ray".
c. "new ⟳" / "old ⟳" — short, matches the spec's "new path"/"old path" wording.

I'd suggest b. It matches the existing pattern.

he checkout line then pulls down just that folder's files.

You end up with a mono folder that contains only lv. If you later want another folder, run `git sparse-checkout set lv di` and it fills in.

One caveat: this still downloads the whole commit history, so on a huge repo it is smaller than a full clone but not tiny. Add `--depth 1` after `--no-checkout` if you also want to skip old history.

ggestions. Same failure as block versus box, paint versus render, drawer versus renderer. Each time I do it I am claiming the project's words are interchangeable with rougher synonyms. They are not, and the rules only work because the distinctions are kept.

The compression and the confirmation bias compounded: the shorter phrase let me drop a distinction I had a hypothesis-shaped reason to drop.

e top of the file's indentation level (no leading spaces), so the numbered list of rules pauses at rule 25 and resumes with rule 26 below the table. cument that the new design today has no manual escape hatch: if the algorithm produces a label position the user does not like, the user has no way to nudge it. If that becomes a real problem after step 3g flips the default, this step formalises an override mechanism (drag-to-reposition or right-click menu). Until then, the lack of an override is acknowledged in writing rather than left implicit.

So the packer is not a one-dimensional sort — it is a constraint solver on a plane: each label has a fixed dim-line direction it can slide along, and the overlap test uses the full rectangle in the plane.

s:
- The slide repair adds inner-loop work, modestly. Dense scenes pay a small per-candidate cost.
- Naming the rule pins the vocabulary; future divergence requires updating proposal AND code together. (Same cost as every formalized rule.)

If you want, the next move after sign-off is to refactor the seven-filter check to expose a "first failing filter" identifier and add the slide-repair branch.

Yes. A candidate is one (edge, uniface, witness index, label position) tuple. Rejecting it just moves the search to the next tuple in the enumeration — a different combination of the four degrees of freedom. The label itself only exists once a candidate wins.

Slide repair is not a numbered filter — it is a controlled retry triggered when filters 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, or 7 reject the candidate. It does not fire for filter 4 (own two witnesses fifteen pixels apart) because that check depends only on the edge endpoints and the chosen uniface, not on the label position. Sliding the label cannot change it.

The existing silhouette box, witness line, and world pass entries that used other phrasings now use the standardized terms.

Simple version:

Right now the placement check says yes or no.

After the change the placement check says yes, or no plus what was in the way.

If what was in the way is something the label can slide past by N pixels, shift the label along the edge by N plus one pixel and try the same spot one more time. If that try passes, keep it. If it still fails, drop it.

That is the whole change.

change the filter pipeline says yes, or no plus the filter that rejected and how many pixels of separation that filter wanted.

If the rejecting filter is one whose rule is a separation distance, shift the label along the edge by that many pixels plus one, and run the filter pipeline on the shifted candidate one more time. Keep it if that pass succeeds, drop it if not.

That is the whole change.

The fix: never trade a rule for a wish.

Nice-to-have: something I should aim for if I can, but never at the cost of breaking a rule. If pursuing it would break a rule, I drop it.

For contrast — rule: something I must not break, no matter what is at stake on the other side.

The whole point of the two words is that one can be given up and the other cannot.

Want me to add both to the lexicon as a paired entry?

The fix is small: try both directions, run the score on each one that clears, and let the existing high-score-wins logic pick between them. Same code shape, just two scored candidates instead of one, when both survive.

olds the cursor in window space (counted from the top-left of the browser window). The line endpoints I compare to are in canvas space (counted from the top-left of the drawing canvas). Different origins, so the hit always misses by the canvas's offset.

Fix: subtract the canvas's top-left from the mouse position before comparing. Then the two coordinate spaces line up.

"Side" — one of the six outward directions of the uniface box (LEFT, RIGHT, FRONT, BACK, TOP, BOTTOM). The dim line and label live on one of these.

"Edge" — one of the four edges of the part being measured that runs along the dimensioned axis. Each box-shaped part has four such edges, one at each corner pair of the other two axes. The two witness lines start at the endpoints of one of these.

Cons:
- The filter pipeline turns into two stages instead of one: shape stage (runs once per edge+side+band) and position stage (runs once per position). Slightly less uniform than one ordered list.
- evaluate_clearances either splits into two helpers (one for shape, one for position), or grows a flag to skip the shape checks on per-position calls. Either way, the test surface gets an extra knob.

l rendered parts fill less of that shape. The "empty room" you see is real space, but it sits inside the imaginary box's projection.

The fix that matches your eye: build the silhouette polygon from the actual rendered geometry, not from the imaginary box. The old path's code already builds per-part convex hulls and unions them. Reusing that would shrink the polygon to fit what is actually drawn.

trl Enter To Send"**.

**Or edit your settings file directly** and add:
```json
"claudeCode.useCtrlEnterToSend": true
```

After that:
- **Return** adds a new line and no longer sends.
- **Cmd+Return** (or Ctrl+Return) sends.
- **Shift+Return** also adds a new line, same as always.

Source: official Claude Code VS Code docs, the extension settings table — https://code.claude.com/docs/en/vs-code

tz, calcite, gypsum — are heavier per unit of space than that bulk clay. Pyrite is far heavier.
- But compared with the pure, dry clay mineral on its own (not the porous bulk), some crystals are about the same or even a touch lighter.

So usually yes, the crystal is denser than the surrounding clay taken as a whole — but that's a consequence of what it's made of, not the reason it grew there.

2. POST each log line to a small endpoint on the dev server that appends to a file on disk — needs a few lines of server code.
3. Accumulate the log in a variable and add a button that downloads it as a text file — pure browser, no server.

not great for reading static text. Two easier ways:

1. Right-click the file → Open With → TextEdit (or Sublime/VS Code if you have one).
2. In Terminal, run `open -a TextEdit foo.log` to open it once, or `open -a "Visual Studio Code" foo.log`.

If you want every `.log` file to open in TextEdit from now on: right-click → Get Info → "Open with" → pick TextEdit → click "Change All…".

quadratic or a "scale to silhouette diagonal" rule is needed.

Three concrete versions to choose from:

A. Big linear bump — change the weight from one per pixel to, say, twenty per pixel.
B. Quadratic — penalty proportional to witness length squared.
C. Relative-to-silhouette — penalty zero until the witness exceeds half the silhouette's screen diagonal, then ramps fast.

Want A, B, or C?

Two ways to use it:

A. Replace the current pixels-inside penalty with this percent penalty.
B. Keep the current pixels-inside penalty AND add this percent penalty.

Want A, B, or a different weight than 20?

he placement loop today iterates by part then by axis; the new sort flattens to a list of (part, axis, value) tuples sorted descending.

The cheaper, less invasive of the two is the sort-first version. It is a one-line change in the part-iteration order plus a documented update to the duplicate-text tiebreak. The displacement version is a real placement-pipeline change and is harder to get right.

pace → assert one pick drops; scene with two parts both viable at the same direction → assert they share the uniface; one part with a very long witness → assert the pick records it without truncation; and the no-cascade tests can compare picks across runs with one part removed.

Together these unlock the remaining eighteen pending tests without changing any placement or rendering behaviour.

ve no canonical replacement.
- File-path and function-name references (Debug.ts, dim_labels, run_uniface_placement) are literal source-code identifiers, not prose — they must stay as-is even if they include non-lexicon substrings.
- A pass that only renames terms without re-thinking the surrounding sentence often leaves an awkward shape: the sentence was built around the original word's rhythm.

the three-system clean shape starts to fray.

Acknowledged on the strict rule — the three never mix. Every arithmetic combination of two numbers from different systems is a bug; every legitimate cross-system use is an explicit, named conversion (mm × scale → px, px ÷ length → %, % × length → px, and no others). The compiler check in level 3 enforces zero tolerance, not "warn if mixed."

ensionless integer multiplied by a px length is a unit error, not a unit conversion.

es, drop that flag.
- `--depth 1` and `--filter=blob:none` make it download less history to save time. Fine to keep if he only wants the latest version, harmless otherwise.

A clean command that just works:
```
git clone https://github.com/gizmolab10/mono.git
```

If download size is a concern and he only needs the latest version:
```
git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/gizmolab10/mono.git
```

is called **Screenshot**. You can launch it, or just use the keyboard:

- **Shift + Command + 4** — crosshairs to drag a box around any part of the screen
- **Shift + Command + 3** — grabs the whole screen at once
- **Shift + Command + 5** — opens a small control bar with options for a region, a window, or a recording, plus where to save

The grabbed image saves to your desktop by default.

Yes — chapter four is the filter chain. Title proposal: "Per part-axis: the filter chain — every candidate in, viable candidates out." Output of the chain is the set of viable candidates that go into chapter five for scoring.

ild inconsistency.
- "Each axis on each part" reads as the cartesian product but technically that is the same as "each (part, axis) pair" — the reading is just verbose for the same idea.

If "each axis on each part" wins for the heading, the sensible follow-on is: replace "part-axis" everywhere else in the spec with "(part, axis) pair" (or "part-and-axis pair"), so the spec speaks one language.

The current placement at 2.1.2 contradicts 2.5 a few lines below it.

the camera produces shorter witnesses than the dim line direction that lies in the wall plane, so the main score picks it before the tiebreaker even reads its value.

If you want the in-plane preference, the rule has to change at the primary level — for example, penalize directions whose world perpendicular has a large dot product with the camera direction, so out-of-plane choices lose ground.
